name: litellm-acompletion
input:
{"messages": [{"role": "system", "content": "You are an ITOps analysis agent with access to Kubernetes tools via MCP.

Your task is to analyze the health of your assigned Kubernetes scope by:
1. Using the available tools to gather information
2. Investigating any issues you discover
3. Producing a final structured analysis

## Your Scope
You operate inside a single Kubernetes namespace: **`sock-shop`**.
The MCP server backing your tools is bound by RBAC to this namespace only.
You MUST stay within this scope:
- Always pass `namespace="sock-shop"` to any tool whose inputSchema declares a `namespace` argument.
- Prefer tools whose schema declares a `namespace` parameter.
- Do NOT call cluster-scoped tools — any tool whose schema does NOT accept `namespace`,
  including cluster-resource listers and node-level resource probes. They will fail
  with RBAC `forbidden`.
- For timeseries queries, pin `{namespace="sock-shop"}` selectors.

## Fallback Doctrine
The full inventory of tools you can call is supplied to you as `tools=[...]`.
Inspect each tool's `name`, `description`, and `inputSchema` before deciding
which one to call — argument names and tool naming vary across MCP servers,
so DO NOT assume any specific tool exists; pick by shape and description.

When a tool returns `forbidden`, do NOT retry it.
When a tool returns `not available` (or a similar "backend missing" error), do
NOT give up: scan your remaining tools and find one that can answer the same
question through a different route (e.g. if a snapshot tool is unavailable,
look for a tool that accepts a query string and ask a timeseries backend).
Only record an observability gap if you have tried every reasonable
alternative tool and all of them have failed.

## Resource Metrics
Primary path — find a tool that returns a per-pod resource snapshot
(CPU / memory) and accepts `namespace="sock-shop"`. Call it, and if it returns rows, use them.

Fallback path — if the primary tool is unavailable, returns `not available`,
or no such tool exists, find a tool that accepts a query string and ask a
Prometheus-compatible timeseries backend. Pin every query to your namespace:
  - CPU per pod:    sum by (pod) (rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"}[2m]))
  - Memory per pod: sum by (pod) (container_memory_working_set_bytes{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"})
  - Net RX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_receive_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
  - Net TX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_transmit_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
    Drop counters are not always emitted; if empty, retry with
    container_network_receive_errors_total / container_network_transmit_errors_total.
  - Pod state:      kube_pod_status_phase{namespace="sock-shop"} == 1
  - Restarts:       kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{namespace="sock-shop"}

Only record `metrics_unavailable` if BOTH paths fail — no snapshot tool AND no
timeseries-query tool can answer, OR every query returns empty for ≥ 2 attempts.

## Chaos Awareness (in-scope, data-driven detection)
You operate inside ONE namespace. You MUST NOT call any tool with a different
`namespace` argument. Before recommending remediation for any symptom, attempt
to determine whether the symptom is being driven by a deliberate disturbance
scheduled into your namespace. Use whatever tools are available to you:

1. Anomalous pod fleet — list pods in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`). The agent has NO inter-scan memory of pod inventory, so checks
   must be evaluable from a single snapshot:
     • pods with ownerReferences whose `kind` is NOT one of
       Deployment, ReplicaSet, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob — anything
       else (custom CRD kind) is suspicious
     • pods with NO ownerRef at all (raw pods are unusual in production namespaces)
     • pods whose containers terminate-and-restart in a tight loop
   Capture the pod name, the unfamiliar owner kind, and any annotation/label
   keys (NOT values — keys are enough to identify the framework at runtime).

2. In-namespace events — list events in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`) and look for reasons containing `Inject`, `Kill`, `Chaos`, `Disrupt`,
   `Fault`, or events referencing pods that no longer exist. Capture reasons verbatim.

3. Metric-backed disturbance probe — only if you have a tool that lists / introspects
   the metrics catalogue AND a tool that runs a query string against it:
   a. Discover available metrics whose name matches `(?i)(chaos|fault|inject|disrupt)`.
      If none, this step is empty.
   b. For each candidate series, read its label set. Pick the label whose name contains
      "namespace" or "ns" — that is the namespace-scoping label. DO NOT assume a
      fixed label name.
   c. Query `<series>{<discovered-label>="sock-shop"}` grouped by every other label.
      A non-empty result means a disturbance is currently targeting your namespace.

If ANY step surfaces evidence, classify the symptom as
`category="external-disturbance"` (or `"chaos-injected"` if a metric-backed step
surfaced matching series — the metric/label values are auditable evidence). Copy the
discovered marker into `summary`. Set `recommended_action` to monitor for recovery
rather than mutate the workload. Do NOT delete or scale the suspicious pods themselves.

If NO step surfaced evidence, treat the symptom as a real fault.

## Dependency Log Drill-Down
After identifying a problem pod, drill down using whatever tools are available:
  - fetch the pod's logs (use a tool that takes a pod `name` plus `namespace`;
    request ~300 lines of tail if a `tail` parameter is supported)
  - fetch the pod's full description (a tool that takes pod `name` + `namespace`).

If the pod's logs reference a connection-refused / no-reachable-servers / timeout
to a hostname `X`:
  - Match `X` against `<service>`, `<service>.sock-shop`, or `<service>.sock-shop.svc.cluster.local`.
    If matched, fetch the Service definition (use a tool that takes service `name`
    + `namespace`), read its `selector`, list pods in `sock-shop` whose labels match the
    selector, then fetch logs for each. All calls stay pinned to `namespace="sock-shop"`.
  - If `X` does not match any of those patterns, treat it as out-of-namespace —
    do NOT attempt to resolve it, do NOT read pods elsewhere; record `X` as an
    external dependency in `summary` and move on.

## When You Have Enough Information
Once you have gathered sufficient data to assess cluster health, provide your final analysis.

## JSON Output Format
Your final response must be a valid JSON object with this structure:
{
  "status_reasoning": {
    "determined_status": "healthy|degraded|critical|unknown",
    "status_justification": ["reason1", "reason2"],
    "data_quality": {
      "completeness": "complete|partial|insufficient",
      "gaps": ["metrics_unavailable", "logs_unavailable", ...],
      "confidence_impact": "Health score capped due to missing observability"
    }
  },
  "thoughts": {
    "key_observations": [],
    "analysis_approach": [],
    "observability_gaps": ["List any tools that failed or data that was unavailable"]
  },
  "health": {
    "total_pods": 0,
    "healthy_pods": 0,
    "unhealthy_pods": 0,
    "error_count": 0,
    "warning_count": 0,
    "overall_health_score": 0
  },
  "issues": [
    {
      "severity": "critical|warning|info",
      "component": "component-name",
      "category": "category",
      "summary": "description",
      "recommended_action": "what to do"
    }
  ],
  "insights": {
    "summary": "overall summary",
    "concerns": [],
    "recommendations": [],
    "observability_recommendations": ["Deploy metrics-server", ...]
  }
}"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Analyze the health of namespace `sock-shop` using only namespace-scoped tools. Task: Analyse the data from MCP tools and provide insights."}], "tools": [{"type": "function", "function": {"name": "configuration_view", "description": "Get the current Kubernetes configuration content as a kubeconfig YAML", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"minified": {"type": "boolean", "description": "Return a minified version of the configuration. If set to true, keeps only the current-context and the relevant pieces of the configuration for that context. If set to false, all contexts, clusters, auth-infos, and users are returned in the configuration. (Optional, default true)"}}}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "events_list", "description": "List Kubernetes events (warnings, errors, state changes) for debugging and troubleshooting in the current cluster from all namespaces", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"namespace": {"type": "string", "description": "Optional Namespace to retrieve the events from. If not provided, will list events from all namespaces"}}}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "namespaces_list", "description": "List all the Kubernetes namespaces in the current cluster", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {}}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "nodes_log", "description": "Get logs from a Kubernetes node (kubelet, kube-proxy, or other system logs). This accesses node logs through the Kubernetes API proxy to the kubelet", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"name": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the node to get logs from"}, "query": {"type": "string", "description": "query specifies services(s) or files from which to return logs (required). Example: "kubelet" to fetch kubelet logs, "/<log-file-name>" to fetch a specific log file from the node (e.g., "/var/log/kubelet.log" or "/var/log/kube-proxy.log")"}, "tailLines": {"type": "integer", "description": "Number of lines to retrieve from the end of the logs (Optional, 0 means all logs)", "default": 100, "minimum": 0}}, "required": ["name", "query"]}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "nodes_stats_summary", "description": "Get detailed resource usage statistics from a Kubernetes node via the kubelet's Summary API. Provides comprehensive metrics including CPU, memory, filesystem, and network usage at the node, pod, and container levels. On systems with cgroup v2 and kernel 4.20+, also includes PSI (Pressure Stall Information) metrics that show resource pressure for CPU, memory, and I/O. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/instrumentation/understand-psi-metrics/ for details on PSI metrics", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"name": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the node to get stats from"}}, "required": ["name"]}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "nodes_top", "description": "List the resource consumption (CPU and memory) as recorded by the Kubernetes Metrics Server for the specified Kubernetes Nodes or all nodes in the cluster", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"label_selector": {"type": "string", "description": "Kubernetes label selector (e.g. 'node-role.kubernetes.io/worker=') to filter nodes by label (Optional, only applicable when name is not provided)", "pattern": "^([/_.\-A-Za-z0-9=, ()!])+$"}, "name": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the Node to get the resource consumption from (Optional, all Nodes if not provided)"}}}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_delete", "description": "Delete a Kubernetes Pod in the current or provided namespace with the provided name", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"name": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the Pod to delete"}, "namespace": {"type": "string", "description": "Namespace to delete the Pod from"}}, "required": ["name"]}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_exec", "description": "Execute a command in a Kubernetes Pod (shell access, run commands in container) in the current or provided namespace with the provided name and command", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"command": {"type": "array", "items": {"type": "string"}, "description": "Command to execute in the Pod container. The first item is the command to be run, and the rest are the arguments to that command. Example: ["ls", "-l", "/tmp"]"}, "container": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the Pod container where the command will be executed (Optional)"}, "name": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the Pod where the command will be executed"}, "namespace": {"type": "string", "description": "Namespace of the Pod where the command will be executed"}}, "required": ["name", "command"]}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_get", "description": "Get a Kubernetes Pod in the current or provided namespace with the provided name", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"name": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the Pod"}, "namespace": {"type": "string", "description": "Namespace to get the Pod from"}}, "required": ["name"]}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_list", "description": "List all the Kubernetes pods in the current cluster from all namespaces", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"fieldSelector": {"type": "string", "description": "Optional Kubernetes field selector to filter pods by field values (e.g. 'status.phase=Running', 'spec.nodeName=node1'). Supported fields: metadata.name, metadata.namespace, spec.nodeName, spec.restartPolicy, spec.schedulerName, spec.serviceAccountName, status.phase (Pending/Running/Succeeded/Failed/Unknown), status.podIP, status.nominatedNodeName. Note: CrashLoopBackOff is a container state, not a pod phase, so it cannot be filtered directly. {"messages": [{"role": "system", "content": "You are an ITOps analysis agent with access to Kubernetes tools via MCP.

Your task is to analyze the health of your assigned Kubernetes scope by:
1. Using the available tools to gather information
2. Investigating any issues you discover
3. Producing a final structured analysis

## Your Scope
You operate inside a single Kubernetes namespace: **`sock-shop`**.
The MCP server backing your tools is bound by RBAC to this namespace only.
You MUST stay within this scope:
- Always pass `namespace="sock-shop"` to any tool whose inputSchema declares a `namespace` argument.
- Prefer tools whose schema declares a `namespace` parameter.
- Do NOT call cluster-scoped tools — any tool whose schema does NOT accept `namespace`,
  including cluster-resource listers and node-level resource probes. They will fail
  with RBAC `forbidden`.
- For timeseries queries, pin `{namespace="sock-shop"}` selectors.

## Fallback Doctrine
The full inventory of tools you can call is supplied to you as `tools=[...]`.
Inspect each tool's `name`, `description`, and `inputSchema` before deciding
which one to call — argument names and tool naming vary across MCP servers,
so DO NOT assume any specific tool exists; pick by shape and description.

When a tool returns `forbidden`, do NOT retry it.
When a tool returns `not available` (or a similar "backend missing" error), do
NOT give up: scan your remaining tools and find one that can answer the same
question through a different route (e.g. if a snapshot tool is unavailable,
look for a tool that accepts a query string and ask a timeseries backend).
Only record an observability gap if you have tried every reasonable
alternative tool and all of them have failed.

## Resource Metrics
Primary path — find a tool that returns a per-pod resource snapshot
(CPU / memory) and accepts `namespace="sock-shop"`. Call it, and if it returns rows, use them.

Fallback path — if the primary tool is unavailable, returns `not available`,
or no such tool exists, find a tool that accepts a query string and ask a
Prometheus-compatible timeseries backend. Pin every query to your namespace:
  - CPU per pod:    sum by (pod) (rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"}[2m]))
  - Memory per pod: sum by (pod) (container_memory_working_set_bytes{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"})
  - Net RX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_receive_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
  - Net TX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_transmit_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
    Drop counters are not always emitted; if empty, retry with
    container_network_receive_errors_total / container_network_transmit_errors_total.
  - Pod state:      kube_pod_status_phase{namespace="sock-shop"} == 1
  - Restarts:       kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{namespace="sock-shop"}

Only record `metrics_unavailable` if BOTH paths fail — no snapshot tool AND no
timeseries-query tool can answer, OR every query returns empty for ≥ 2 attempts.

## Chaos Awareness (in-scope, data-driven detection)
You operate inside ONE namespace. You MUST NOT call any tool with a different
`namespace` argument. Before recommending remediation for any symptom, attempt
to determine whether the symptom is being driven by a deliberate disturbance
scheduled into your namespace. Use whatever tools are available to you:

1. Anomalous pod fleet — list pods in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`). The agent has NO inter-scan memory of pod inventory, so checks
   must be evaluable from a single snapshot:
     • pods with ownerReferences whose `kind` is NOT one of
       Deployment, ReplicaSet, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob — anything
       else (custom CRD kind) is suspicious
     • pods with NO ownerRef at all (raw pods are unusual in production namespaces)
     • pods whose containers terminate-and-restart in a tight loop
   Capture the pod name, the unfamiliar owner kind, and any annotation/label
   keys (NOT values — keys are enough to identify the framework at runtime).

2. In-namespace events — list events in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`) and look for reasons containing `Inject`, `Kill`, `Chaos`, `Disrupt`,
   `Fault`, or events referencing pods that no longer exist. Capture reasons verbatim.

3. Metric-backed disturbance probe — only if you have a tool that lists / introspects
   the metrics catalogue AND a tool that runs a query string against it:
   a. Discover available metrics whose name matches `(?i)(chaos|fault|inject|disrupt)`.
      If none, this step is empty.
   b. For each candidate series, read its label set. Pick the label whose name contains
      "namespace" or "ns" — that is the namespace-scoping label. DO NOT assume a
      fixed label name.
   c. Query `<series>{<discovered-label>="sock-shop"}` grouped by every other label.
      A non-empty result means a disturbance is currently targeting your namespace.

If ANY step surfaces evidence, classify the symptom as
`category="external-disturbance"` (or `"chaos-injected"` if a metric-backed step
surfaced matching series — the metric/label values are auditable evidence). Copy the
discovered marker into `summary`. Set `recommended_action` to monitor for recovery
rather than mutate the workload. Do NOT delete or scale the suspicious pods themselves.

If NO step surfaced evidence, treat the symptom as a real fault.

## Dependency Log Drill-Down
After identifying a problem pod, drill down using whatever tools are available:
  - fetch the pod's logs (use a tool that takes a pod `name` plus `namespace`;
    request ~300 lines of tail if a `tail` parameter is supported)
  - fetch the pod's full description (a tool that takes pod `name` + `namespace`).

If the pod's logs reference a connection-refused / no-reachable-servers / timeout
to a hostname `X`:
  - Match `X` against `<service>`, `<service>.sock-shop`, or `<service>.sock-shop.svc.cluster.local`.
    If matched, fetch the Service definition (use a tool that takes service `name`
    + `namespace`), read its `selector`, list pods in `sock-shop` whose labels match the
    selector, then fetch logs for each. All calls stay pinned to `namespace="sock-shop"`.
  - If `X` does not match any of those patterns, treat it as out-of-namespace —
    do NOT attempt to resolve it, do NOT read pods elsewhere; record `X` as an
    external dependency in `summary` and move on.

## When You Have Enough Information
Once you have gathered sufficient data to assess cluster health, provide your final analysis.

## JSON Output Format
Your final response must be a valid JSON object with this structure:
{
  "status_reasoning": {
    "determined_status": "healthy|degraded|critical|unknown",
    "status_justification": ["reason1", "reason2"],
    "data_quality": {
      "completeness": "complete|partial|insufficient",
      "gaps": ["metrics_unavailable", "logs_unavailable", ...],
      "confidence_impact": "Health score capped due to missing observability"
    }
  },
  "thoughts": {
    "key_observations": [],
    "analysis_approach": [],
    "observability_gaps": ["List any tools that failed or data that was unavailable"]
  },
  "health": {
    "total_pods": 0,
    "healthy_pods": 0,
    "unhealthy_pods": 0,
    "error_count": 0,
    "warning_count": 0,
    "overall_health_score": 0
  },
  "issues": [
    {
      "severity": "critical|warning|info",
      "component": "component-name",
      "category": "category",
      "summary": "description",
      "recommended_action": "what to do"
    }
  ],
  "insights": {
    "summary": "overall summary",
    "concerns": [],
    "recommendations": [],
    "observability_recommendations": ["Deploy metrics-server", ...]
  }
}"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Analyze the health of namespace `sock-shop` using only namespace-scoped tools. name: litellm-acompletion
input:
{"messages": [{"role": "system", "content": "You are an ITOps analysis agent with access to Kubernetes tools via MCP.

Your task is to analyze the health of your assigned Kubernetes scope by:
1. Using the available tools to gather information
2. Investigating any issues you discover
3. Producing a final structured analysis

## Your Scope
You operate inside a single Kubernetes namespace: **`sock-shop`**.
The MCP server backing your tools is bound by RBAC to this namespace only.
You MUST stay within this scope:
- Always pass `namespace="sock-shop"` to any tool whose inputSchema declares a `namespace` argument.
- Prefer tools whose schema declares a `namespace` parameter.
- Do NOT call cluster-scoped tools — any tool whose schema does NOT accept `namespace`,
  including cluster-resource listers and node-level resource probes. They will fail
  with RBAC `forbidden`.
- For timeseries queries, pin `{namespace="sock-shop"}` selectors.

## Fallback Doctrine
The full inventory of tools you can call is supplied to you as `tools=[...]`.
Inspect each tool's `name`, `description`, and `inputSchema` before deciding
which one to call — argument names and tool naming vary across MCP servers,
so DO NOT assume any specific tool exists; pick by shape and description.

When a tool returns `forbidden`, do NOT retry it.
When a tool returns `not available` (or a similar "backend missing" error), do
NOT give up: scan your remaining tools and find one that can answer the same
question through a different route (e.g. if a snapshot tool is unavailable,
look for a tool that accepts a query string and ask a timeseries backend).
Only record an observability gap if you have tried every reasonable
alternative tool and all of them have failed.

## Resource Metrics
Primary path — find a tool that returns a per-pod resource snapshot
(CPU / memory) and accepts `namespace="sock-shop"`. Call it, and if it returns rows, use them.

Fallback path — if the primary tool is unavailable, returns `not available`,
or no such tool exists, find a tool that accepts a query string and ask a
Prometheus-compatible timeseries backend. Pin every query to your namespace:
  - CPU per pod:    sum by (pod) (rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"}[2m]))
  - Memory per pod: sum by (pod) (container_memory_working_set_bytes{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"})
  - Net RX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_receive_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
  - Net TX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_transmit_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
    Drop counters are not always emitted; if empty, retry with
    container_network_receive_errors_total / container_network_transmit_errors_total.
  - Pod state:      kube_pod_status_phase{namespace="sock-shop"} == 1
  - Restarts:       kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{namespace="sock-shop"}

Only record `metrics_unavailable` if BOTH paths fail — no snapshot tool AND no
timeseries-query tool can answer, OR every query returns empty for ≥ 2 attempts.

## Chaos Awareness (in-scope, data-driven detection)
You operate inside ONE namespace. You MUST NOT call any tool with a different
`namespace` argument. Before recommending remediation for any symptom, attempt
to determine whether the symptom is being driven by a deliberate disturbance
scheduled into your namespace. Use whatever tools are available to you:

1. Anomalous pod fleet — list pods in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`). The agent has NO inter-scan memory of pod inventory, so checks
   must be evaluable from a single snapshot:
     • pods with ownerReferences whose `kind` is NOT one of
       Deployment, ReplicaSet, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob — anything
       else (custom CRD kind) is suspicious
     • pods with NO ownerRef at all (raw pods are unusual in production namespaces)
     • pods whose containers terminate-and-restart in a tight loop
   Capture the pod name, the unfamiliar owner kind, and any annotation/label
   keys (NOT values — keys are enough to identify the framework at runtime).

2. In-namespace events — list events in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`) and look for reasons containing `Inject`, `Kill`, `Chaos`, `Disrupt`,
   `Fault`, or events referencing pods that no longer exist. Capture reasons verbatim.

3. Metric-backed disturbance probe — only if you have a tool that lists / introspects
   the metrics catalogue AND a tool that runs a query string against it:
   a. Discover available metrics whose name matches `(?i)(chaos|fault|inject|disrupt)`.
      If none, this step is empty.
   b. For each candidate series, read its label set. Pick the label whose name contains
      "namespace" or "ns" — that is the namespace-scoping label. DO NOT assume a
      fixed label name.
   c. Query `<series>{<discovered-label>="sock-shop"}` grouped by every other label.
      A non-empty result means a disturbance is currently targeting your namespace.

If ANY step surfaces evidence, classify the symptom as
`category="external-disturbance"` (or `"chaos-injected"` if a metric-backed step
surfaced matching series — the metric/label values are auditable evidence). Copy the
discovered marker into `summary`. Set `recommended_action` to monitor for recovery
rather than mutate the workload. Do NOT delete or scale the suspicious pods themselves.

If NO step surfaced evidence, treat the symptom as a real fault.

## Dependency Log Drill-Down
After identifying a problem pod, drill down using whatever tools are available:
  - fetch the pod's logs (use a tool that takes a pod `name` plus `namespace`;
    request ~300 lines of tail if a `tail` parameter is supported)
  - fetch the pod's full description (a tool that takes pod `name` + `namespace`).

If the pod's logs reference a connection-refused / no-reachable-servers / timeout
to a hostname `X`:
  - Match `X` against `<service>`, `<service>.sock-shop`, or `<service>.sock-shop.svc.cluster.local`.
    If matched, fetch the Service definition (use a tool that takes service `name`
    + `namespace`), read its `selector`, list pods in `sock-shop` whose labels match the
    selector, then fetch logs for each. All calls stay pinned to `namespace="sock-shop"`.
  - If `X` does not match any of those patterns, treat it as out-of-namespace —
    do NOT attempt to resolve it, do NOT read pods elsewhere; record `X` as an
    external dependency in `summary` and move on.

## When You Have Enough Information
Once you have gathered sufficient data to assess cluster health, provide your final analysis.

## JSON Output Format
Your final response must be a valid JSON object with this structure:
{
  "status_reasoning": {
    "determined_status": "healthy|degraded|critical|unknown",
    "status_justification": ["reason1", "reason2"],
    "data_quality": {
      "completeness": "complete|partial|insufficient",
      "gaps": ["metrics_unavailable", "logs_unavailable", ...],
      "confidence_impact": "Health score capped due to missing observability"
    }
  },
  "thoughts": {
    "key_observations": [],
    "analysis_approach": [],
    "observability_gaps": ["List any tools that failed or data that was unavailable"]
  },
  "health": {
    "total_pods": 0,
    "healthy_pods": 0,
    "unhealthy_pods": 0,
    "error_count": 0,
    "warning_count": 0,
    "overall_health_score": 0
  },
  "issues": [
    {
      "severity": "critical|warning|info",
      "component": "component-name",
      "category": "category",
      "summary": "description",
      "recommended_action": "what to do"
    }
  ],
  "insights": {
    "summary": "overall summary",
    "concerns": [],
    "recommendations": [],
    "observability_recommendations": ["Deploy metrics-server", ...]
  }
}"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Analyze the health of namespace `sock-shop` using only namespace-scoped tools. Task: Analyse the data from MCP tools and provide insights."}], "tools": [{"type": "function", "function": {"name": "configuration_view", "description": "Get the current Kubernetes configuration content as a kubeconfig YAML", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"minified": {"type": "boolean", "description": "Return a minified version of the configuration. If set to true, keeps only the current-context and the relevant pieces of the configuration for that context. If set to false, all contexts, clusters, auth-infos, and users are returned in the configuration. (Optional, default true)"}}}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "events_list", "description": "List Kubernetes events (warnings, errors, state changes) for debugging and troubleshooting in the current cluster from all namespaces", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"namespace": {"type": "string", "description": "Optional Namespace to retrieve the events from. If not provided, will list events from all namespaces"}}}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "namespaces_list", "description": "List all the Kubernetes namespaces in the current cluster", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {}}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "nodes_log", "description": "Get logs from a Kubernetes node (kubelet, kube-proxy, or other system logs). This accesses node logs through the Kubernetes API proxy to the kubelet", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"name": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the node to get logs from"}, "query": {"type": "string", "description": "query specifies services(s) or files from which to return logs (required). Example: "kubelet" to fetch kubelet logs, "/<log-file-name>" to fetch a specific log file from the node (e.g., "/var/log/kubelet.log" or "/var/log/kube-proxy.log")"}, "tailLines": {"type": "integer", "description": "Number of lines to retrieve from the end of the logs (Optional, 0 means all logs)", "default": 100, "minimum": 0}}, "required": ["name", "query"]}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "nodes_stats_summary", "description": "Get detailed resource usage statistics from a Kubernetes node via the kubelet's Summary API. Provides comprehensive metrics including CPU, memory, filesystem, and network usage at the node, pod, and container levels. On systems with cgroup v2 and kernel 4.20+, also includes PSI (Pressure Stall Information) metrics that show resource pressure for CPU, memory, and I/O. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/instrumentation/understand-psi-metrics/ for details on PSI metrics", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"name": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the node to get stats from"}}, "required": ["name"]}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "nodes_top", "description": "List the resource consumption (CPU and memory) as recorded by the Kubernetes Metrics Server for the specified Kubernetes Nodes or all nodes in the cluster", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"label_selector": {"type": "string", "description": "Kubernetes label selector (e.g. 'node-role.kubernetes.io/worker=') to filter nodes by label (Optional, only applicable when name is not provided)", "pattern": "^([/_.\-A-Za-z0-9=, ()!])+$"}, "name": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the Node to get the resource consumption from (Optional, all Nodes if not provided)"}}}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_delete", "description": "Delete a Kubernetes Pod in the current or provided namespace with the provided name", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"name": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the Pod to delete"}, "namespace": {"type": "string", "description": "Namespace to delete the Pod from"}}, "required": ["name"]}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_exec", "description": "Execute a command in a Kubernetes Pod (shell access, run commands in container) in the current or provided namespace with the provided name and command", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"command": {"type": "array", "items": {"type": "string"}, "description": "Command to execute in the Pod container. The first item is the command to be run, and the rest are the arguments to that command. Example: ["ls", "-l", "/tmp"]"}, "container": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the Pod container where the command will be executed (Optional)"}, "name": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the Pod where the command will be executed"}, "namespace": {"type": "string", "description": "Namespace of the Pod where the command will be executed"}}, "required": ["name", "command"]}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_get", "description": "Get a Kubernetes Pod in the current or provided namespace with the provided name", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"name": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the Pod"}, "namespace": {"type": "string", "description": "Namespace to get the Pod from"}}, "required": ["name"]}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_list", "description": "List all the Kubernetes pods in the current cluster from all namespaces", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"fieldSelector": {"type": "string", "description": "Optional Kubernetes field selector to filter pods by field values (e.g. 'status.phase=Running', 'spec.nodeName=node1'). {"messages": [{"role": "system", "content": "You are an ITOps analysis agent with access to Kubernetes tools via MCP.

Your task is to analyze the health of your assigned Kubernetes scope by:
1. Using the available tools to gather information
2. Investigating any issues you discover
3. Producing a final structured analysis

## Your Scope
You operate inside a single Kubernetes namespace: **`sock-shop`**.
The MCP server backing your tools is bound by RBAC to this namespace only.
You MUST stay within this scope:
- Always pass `namespace="sock-shop"` to any tool whose inputSchema declares a `namespace` argument.
- Prefer tools whose schema declares a `namespace` parameter.
- Do NOT call cluster-scoped tools — any tool whose schema does NOT accept `namespace`,
  including cluster-resource listers and node-level resource probes. They will fail
  with RBAC `forbidden`.
- For timeseries queries, pin `{namespace="sock-shop"}` selectors.

## Fallback Doctrine
The full inventory of tools you can call is supplied to you as `tools=[...]`.
Inspect each tool's `name`, `description`, and `inputSchema` before deciding
which one to call — argument names and tool naming vary across MCP servers,
so DO NOT assume any specific tool exists; pick by shape and description.

When a tool returns `forbidden`, do NOT retry it.
When a tool returns `not available` (or a similar "backend missing" error), do
NOT give up: scan your remaining tools and find one that can answer the same
question through a different route (e.g. if a snapshot tool is unavailable,
look for a tool that accepts a query string and ask a timeseries backend).
Only record an observability gap if you have tried every reasonable
alternative tool and all of them have failed.

## Resource Metrics
Primary path — find a tool that returns a per-pod resource snapshot
(CPU / memory) and accepts `namespace="sock-shop"`. Call it, and if it returns rows, use them.

Fallback path — if the primary tool is unavailable, returns `not available`,
or no such tool exists, find a tool that accepts a query string and ask a
Prometheus-compatible timeseries backend. Pin every query to your namespace:
  - CPU per pod:    sum by (pod) (rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"}[2m]))
  - Memory per pod: sum by (pod) (container_memory_working_set_bytes{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"})
  - Net RX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_receive_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
  - Net TX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_transmit_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
    Drop counters are not always emitted; if empty, retry with
    container_network_receive_errors_total / container_network_transmit_errors_total.
  - Pod state:      kube_pod_status_phase{namespace="sock-shop"} == 1
  - Restarts:       kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{namespace="sock-shop"}

Only record `metrics_unavailable` if BOTH paths fail — no snapshot tool AND no
timeseries-query tool can answer, OR every query returns empty for ≥ 2 attempts.

## Chaos Awareness (in-scope, data-driven detection)
You operate inside ONE namespace. You MUST NOT call any tool with a different
`namespace` argument. Before recommending remediation for any symptom, attempt
to determine whether the symptom is being driven by a deliberate disturbance
scheduled into your namespace. Use whatever tools are available to you:

1. Anomalous pod fleet — list pods in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`). The agent has NO inter-scan memory of pod inventory, so checks
   must be evaluable from a single snapshot:
     • pods with ownerReferences whose `kind` is NOT one of
       Deployment, ReplicaSet, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob — anything
       else (custom CRD kind) is suspicious
     • pods with NO ownerRef at all (raw pods are unusual in production namespaces)
     • pods whose containers terminate-and-restart in a tight loop
   Capture the pod name, the unfamiliar owner kind, and any annotation/label
   keys (NOT values — keys are enough to identify the framework at runtime).

2. In-namespace events — list events in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`) and look for reasons containing `Inject`, `Kill`, `Chaos`, `Disrupt`,
   `Fault`, or events referencing pods that no longer exist. Capture reasons verbatim.

3. Metric-backed disturbance probe — only if you have a tool that lists / introspects
   the metrics catalogue AND a tool that runs a query string against it:
   a. Discover available metrics whose name matches `(?i)(chaos|fault|inject|disrupt)`.
      If none, this step is empty.
   b. For each candidate series, read its label set. Pick the label whose name contains
      "namespace" or "ns" — that is the namespace-scoping label. DO NOT assume a
      fixed label name.
   c. Query `<series>{<discovered-label>="sock-shop"}` grouped by every other label.
      A non-empty result means a disturbance is currently targeting your namespace.

If ANY step surfaces evidence, classify the symptom as
`category="external-disturbance"` (or `"chaos-injected"` if a metric-backed step
surfaced matching series — the metric/label values are auditable evidence). Copy the
discovered marker into `summary`. Set `recommended_action` to monitor for recovery
rather than mutate the workload. Do NOT delete or scale the suspicious pods themselves.

If NO step surfaced evidence, treat the symptom as a real fault.

## Dependency Log Drill-Down
After identifying a problem pod, drill down using whatever tools are available:
  - fetch the pod's logs (use a tool that takes a pod `name` plus `namespace`;
    request ~300 lines of tail if a `tail` parameter is supported)
  - fetch the pod's full description (a tool that takes pod `name` + `namespace`).

If the pod's logs reference a connection-refused / no-reachable-servers / timeout
to a hostname `X`:
  - Match `X` against `<service>`, `<service>.sock-shop`, or `<service>.sock-shop.svc.cluster.local`.
    If matched, fetch the Service definition (use a tool that takes service `name`
    + `namespace`), read its `selector`, list pods in `sock-shop` whose labels match the
    selector, then fetch logs for each. All calls stay pinned to `namespace="sock-shop"`.
  - If `X` does not match any of those patterns, treat it as out-of-namespace —
    do NOT attempt to resolve it, do NOT read pods elsewhere; record `X` as an
    external dependency in `summary` and move on.

## When You Have Enough Information
Once you have gathered sufficient data to assess cluster health, provide your final analysis.

## JSON Output Format
Your final response must be a valid JSON object with this structure:
{
  "status_reasoning": {
    "determined_status": "healthy|degraded|critical|unknown",
    "status_justification": ["reason1", "reason2"],
    "data_quality": {
      "completeness": "complete|partial|insufficient",
      "gaps": ["metrics_unavailable", "logs_unavailable", ...],
      "confidence_impact": "Health score capped due to missing observability"
    }
  },
  "thoughts": {
    "key_observations": [],
    "analysis_approach": [],
    "observability_gaps": ["List any tools that failed or data that was unavailable"]
  },
  "health": {
    "total_pods": 0,
    "healthy_pods": 0,
    "unhealthy_pods": 0,
    "error_count": 0,
    "warning_count": 0,
    "overall_health_score": 0
  },
  "issues": [
    {
      "severity": "critical|warning|info",
      "component": "component-name",
      "category": "category",
      "summary": "description",
      "recommended_action": "what to do"
    }
  ],
  "insights": {
    "summary": "overall summary",
    "concerns": [],
    "recommendations": [],
    "observability_recommendations": ["Deploy metrics-server", ...]
  }
}"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Analyze the health of namespace `sock-shop` using only namespace-scoped tools. Task: Analyse the data from MCP tools and provide insights."}, {"role": "assistant", "content": null, "tool_calls": [{"id": "call_ZpFFGa3ntWlHCYDv7UhJ7X7g", "type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_list_in_namespace", "arguments": "{"namespace": "sock-shop"}"}}, {"id": "call_7BKLASz6CpMLHuWQRRAzc4NJ", "type": "function", "function": {"name": "events_list", "arguments": "{"namespace": "sock-shop"}"}}, {"id": "call_YmnlvvS4fBt7rMby4Dg6zUzw", "type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_top", "arguments": "{"all_namespaces": false, "namespace": "sock-shop"}"}}, {"id": "call_HigJusnh3vfKWSFavNtcPNam", "type": "function", "function": {"name": "list_metrics", "arguments": "{"limit": 200, "filter_pattern": "chaos|fault|inject|disrupt", "refresh_cache": false}"}}]}, {"role": "tool", "tool_call_id": "call_ZpFFGa3ntWlHCYDv7UhJ7X7g", "content": "NAMESPACE   APIVERSION   KIND   NAME                                     READY   STATUS    RESTARTS       AGE   IP             NODE                                NOMINATED NODE   READINESS GATES   LABELS

Check carts pod logs around startup for slow dependency initialization (e.g., carts-db) or application-level errors."
    },
    {
      "severity": "warning",
      "component": "user",
      "category": "transient-restart",
      "summary": "user pod shows a non-zero restart count (1 restart) despite currently being Running and Ready.",
      "recommended_action": "Inspect recent logs for the user pod (including previous container logs if available) to determine the cause of the restart. If the restart was due to an OOMKill or crash, consider adjusting resource requests/limits or fixing the underlying application error."
    },
    {
      "severity": "warning",
      "component": "flash-agent",
      "category": "external-disturbance",
      "summary": "flash-agent pod (app=flash-agent) is running in the sock-shop namespace with 2 containers and 2 restarts, suggesting an injected agent or disturbance tool rather than a core sock-shop microservice.",
      "recommended_action": "Confirm with platform/SRE or chaos-engineering owners whether flash-agent is an authorized agent or chaos tool targeting sock-shop. If it is part of a planned experiment, monitor application behavior and avoid manual remediation that interferes with the test. If it is not expected, review its Deployment/owner, RBAC, and configuration to ensure it is not unintentionally impacting the namespace."
    },
    {
      "severity": "info",
      "component": "sock-shop-namespace",
      "category": "observability",
      "summary": "Per-pod resource metrics via metrics.k8s.io are not accessible to the MCP service account, and Prometheus metric catalogue tools are unavailable.",
      "recommended_action": "Grant the MCP service account read access to pods.metrics.k8s.io in namespace sock-shop (or cluster-wide if appropriate), or deploy/enable a metrics adapter that the MCP tools can query. Additionally, expose a Prometheus-compatible metric listing/query interface to improve visibility into CPU, memory, and potential chaos-related metrics."
    }
  ],
  "insights": {
    "summary": "Namespace sock-shop is generally healthy with all pods Running and Ready, but there are signs of mild instability (startup probe failures on carts, single restarts on user and multiple restarts on flash-agent) and the presence of an agent-like flash-agent pod that likely represents an external disturbance or experiment. Observability is limited by lack of metrics-server access and Prometheus catalogue tools.",
    "concerns": [
      "Transient startup probe failures on the carts service could indicate tight probe timing or slow initialization, which may cause intermittent availability issues during rollouts or node restarts.",
      "Non-zero restarts on user and flash-agent pods suggest occasional crashes or restarts that should be understood, especially if they correlate with user-facing errors.",
      "The flash-agent pod appears to be an injected agent or experiment tool; if misconfigured or unexpected, it could introduce latency, faults, or other disturbances into the sock-shop workload.",
      "Lack of direct access to resource metrics and chaos-related metrics reduces the ability to quantify performance, saturation, and the impact of any experiments."
    ],
    "recommendations": [
      "For carts: Review and tune startupProbe and livenessProbe settings. Consider increasing initialDelaySeconds or failureThreshold if the application legitimately takes longer to start, and verify that the /health endpoint is lightweight and available as early as possible.",
      "For user: Pull recent logs (including previous container logs) to identify the cause of the single restart. If it was due to OOM, adjust memory requests/limits; if due to application error, prioritize a fix to prevent recurrence.",
      "For flash-agent: Identify the owning Deployment/ReplicaSet and confirm its purpose. If it is a chaos or observability agent, document its expected behavior (including restarts) and ensure it is not overloading the services. If it is not expected, consider cordoning it from production traffic or moving it to a dedicated experiment namespace.",
      "Implement a lightweight periodic check that correlates pod restarts and warning events with application-level SLOs (e.g., front-end error rates) to detect if these transient issues are user-impacting.",
      "Establish a standard for keeping non-application agents (like flash-agent, MCP servers, Prometheus MCP) clearly labeled and documented so that their presence in application namespaces is intentional and auditable."
    ],
    "observability_recommendations": [
      "Update RBAC to allow the MCP service account (system:serviceaccount:litmus:mcp-server) to list pod metrics from metrics.k8s.io in namespace sock-shop, enabling pods_top or equivalent tools.",
      "Expose or register a Prometheus metric listing and query tool in the MCP server so that chaos/fault/inject/disrupt metrics can be discovered and queried for this namespace.",
      "Add alerts on Kubernetes events for repeated Unhealthy probe failures and elevated restart counts per pod in namespace sock-shop, so that transient issues like those seen on carts and user are surfaced quickly.",
      "Consider enabling application-level metrics (HTTP error rates, latency, dependency errors) for core services (front-end, carts, orders, payment, shipping, user) and ensure they are scraped into Prometheus for correlation with pod-level events."
    ]
  }

name: litellm-acompletion
input:
{"messages": [{"role": "system", "content": "You are an ITOps analysis agent with access to Kubernetes tools via MCP.

Your task is to analyze the health of your assigned Kubernetes scope by:
1. Using the available tools to gather information
2. Investigating any issues you discover
3. Producing a final structured analysis

## Your Scope
You operate inside a single Kubernetes namespace: **`sock-shop`**.
The MCP server backing your tools is bound by RBAC to this namespace only.
You MUST stay within this scope:
- Always pass `namespace="sock-shop"` to any tool whose inputSchema declares a `namespace` argument.
- Prefer tools whose schema declares a `namespace` parameter.
- Do NOT call cluster-scoped tools — any tool whose schema does NOT accept `namespace`,
  including cluster-resource listers and node-level resource probes. They will fail
  with RBAC `forbidden`.
- For timeseries queries, pin `{namespace="sock-shop"}` selectors.

## Fallback Doctrine
The full inventory of tools you can call is supplied to you as `tools=[...]`.
Inspect each tool's `name`, `description`, and `inputSchema` before deciding
which one to call — argument names and tool naming vary across MCP servers,
so DO NOT assume any specific tool exists; pick by shape and description.

When a tool returns `forbidden`, do NOT retry it.
When a tool returns `not available` (or a similar "backend missing" error), do
NOT give up: scan your remaining tools and find one that can answer the same
question through a different route (e.g. if a snapshot tool is unavailable,
look for a tool that accepts a query string and ask a timeseries backend).
Only record an observability gap if you have tried every reasonable
alternative tool and all of them have failed.

## Resource Metrics
Primary path — find a tool that returns a per-pod resource snapshot
(CPU / memory) and accepts `namespace="sock-shop"`. Call it, and if it returns rows, use them.

Fallback path — if the primary tool is unavailable, returns `not available`,
or no such tool exists, find a tool that accepts a query string and ask a
Prometheus-compatible timeseries backend. Pin every query to your namespace:
  - CPU per pod:    sum by (pod) (rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"}[2m]))
  - Memory per pod: sum by (pod) (container_memory_working_set_bytes{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"})
  - Net RX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_receive_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
  - Net TX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_transmit_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
    Drop counters are not always emitted; if empty, retry with
    container_network_receive_errors_total / container_network_transmit_errors_total.
  - Pod state:      kube_pod_status_phase{namespace="sock-shop"} == 1
  - Restarts:       kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{namespace="sock-shop"}

Only record `metrics_unavailable` if BOTH paths fail — no snapshot tool AND no
timeseries-query tool can answer, OR every query returns empty for ≥ 2 attempts.

## Chaos Awareness (in-scope, data-driven detection)
You operate inside ONE namespace. You MUST NOT call any tool with a different
`namespace` argument. Before recommending remediation for any symptom, attempt
to determine whether the symptom is being driven by a deliberate disturbance
scheduled into your namespace. Use whatever tools are available to you:

1. Anomalous pod fleet — list pods in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`). The agent has NO inter-scan memory of pod inventory, so checks
   must be evaluable from a single snapshot:
     • pods with ownerReferences whose `kind` is NOT one of
       Deployment, ReplicaSet, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob — anything
       else (custom CRD kind) is suspicious
     • pods with NO ownerRef at all (raw pods are unusual in production namespaces)
     • pods whose containers terminate-and-restart in a tight loop
   Capture the pod name, the unfamiliar owner kind, and any annotation/label
   keys (NOT values — keys are enough to identify the framework at runtime).

2. In-namespace events — list events in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`) and look for reasons containing `Inject`, `Kill`, `Chaos`, `Disrupt`,
   `Fault`, or events referencing pods that no longer exist. Capture reasons verbatim.

3. Metric-backed disturbance probe — only if you have a tool that lists / introspects
   the metrics catalogue AND a tool that runs a query string against it:
   a. Discover available metrics whose name matches `(?i)(chaos|fault|inject|disrupt)`.
      If none, this step is empty.
   b. For each candidate series, read its label set. Pick the label whose name contains
      "namespace" or "ns" — that is the namespace-scoping label. DO NOT assume a
      fixed label name.
   c. Query `<series>{<discovered-label>="sock-shop"}` grouped by every other label.
      A non-empty result means a disturbance is currently targeting your namespace.

If ANY step surfaces evidence, classify the symptom as
`category="external-disturbance"` (or `"chaos-injected"` if a metric-backed step
surfaced matching series — the metric/label values are auditable evidence). Copy the
discovered marker into `summary`. Set `recommended_action` to monitor for recovery
rather than mutate the workload. Do NOT delete or scale the suspicious pods themselves.

If NO step surfaced evidence, treat the symptom as a real fault.

## Dependency Log Drill-Down
After identifying a problem pod, drill down using whatever tools are available:
  - fetch the pod's logs (use a tool that takes a pod `name` plus `namespace`;
    request ~300 lines of tail if a `tail` parameter is supported)
  - fetch the pod's full description (a tool that takes pod `name` + `namespace`).

If the pod's logs reference a connection-refused / no-reachable-servers / timeout
to a hostname `X`:
  - Match `X` against `<service>`, `<service>.sock-shop`, or `<service>.sock-shop.svc.cluster.local`.
    If matched, fetch the Service definition (use a tool that takes service `name`
    + `namespace`), read its `selector`, list pods in `sock-shop` whose labels match the
    selector, then fetch logs for each. All calls stay pinned to `namespace="sock-shop"`.
  - If `X` does not match any of those patterns, treat it as out-of-namespace —
    do NOT attempt to resolve it, do NOT read pods elsewhere; record `X` as an
    external dependency in `summary` and move on.

## When You Have Enough Information
Once you have gathered sufficient data to assess cluster health, provide your final analysis.

## JSON Output Format
Your final response must be a valid JSON object with this structure:
{
  "status_reasoning": {
    "determined_status": "healthy|degraded|critical|unknown",
    "status_justification": ["reason1", "reason2"],
    "data_quality": {
      "completeness": "complete|partial|insufficient",
      "gaps": ["metrics_unavailable", "logs_unavailable", ...],
      "confidence_impact": "Health score capped due to missing observability"
    }
  },
  "thoughts": {
    "key_observations": [],
    "analysis_approach": [],
    "observability_gaps": ["List any tools that failed or data that was unavailable"]
  },
  "health": {
    "total_pods": 0,
    "healthy_pods": 0,
    "unhealthy_pods": 0,
    "error_count": 0,
    "warning_count": 0,
    "overall_health_score": 0
  },
  "issues": [
    {
      "severity": "critical|warning|info",
      "component": "component-name",
      "category": "category",
      "summary": "description",
      "recommended_action": "what to do"
    }
  ],
  "insights": {
    "summary": "overall summary",
    "concerns": [],
    "recommendations": [],
    "observability_recommendations": ["Deploy metrics-server", ...]
  }
}"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Analyze the health of namespace `sock-shop` using only namespace-scoped tools. Task: Analyse the data from MCP tools and provide insights."}], "tools": [{"type": "function", "function": {"name": "configuration_view", "description": "Get the current Kubernetes configuration content as a kubeconfig YAML", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"minified": {"type": "boolean", "description": "Return a minified version of the configuration. If set to true, keeps only the current-context and the relevant pieces of the configuration for that context. If set to false, all contexts, clusters, auth-infos, and users are returned in the configuration. (Optional, default true)"}}}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "events_list", "description": "List Kubernetes events (warnings, errors, state changes) for debugging and troubleshooting in the current cluster from all namespaces", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"namespace": {"type": "string", "description": "Optional Namespace to retrieve the events from. If not provided, will list events from all namespaces"}}}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "namespaces_list", "description": "List all the Kubernetes namespaces in the current cluster", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {}}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "nodes_log", "description": "Get logs from a Kubernetes node (kubelet, kube-proxy, or other system logs). This accesses node logs through the Kubernetes API proxy to the kubelet", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"name": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the node to get logs from"}, "query": {"type": "string", "description": "query specifies services(s) or files from which to return logs (required). Example: "kubelet" to fetch kubelet logs, "/<log-file-name>" to fetch a specific log file from the node (e.g., "/var/log/kubelet.log" or "/var/log/kube-proxy.log")"}, "tailLines": {"type": "integer", "description": "Number of lines to retrieve from the end of the logs (Optional, 0 means all logs)", "default": 100, "minimum": 0}}, "required": ["name", "query"]}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "nodes_stats_summary", "description": "Get detailed resource usage statistics from a Kubernetes node via the kubelet's Summary API. Provides comprehensive metrics including CPU, memory, filesystem, and network usage at the node, pod, and container levels. On systems with cgroup v2 and kernel 4.20+, also includes PSI (Pressure Stall Information) metrics that show resource pressure for CPU, memory, and I/O. See https://kubernetes.io/docs/reference/instrumentation/understand-psi-metrics/ for details on PSI metrics", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"name": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the node to get stats from"}}, "required": ["name"]}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "nodes_top", "description": "List the resource consumption (CPU and memory) as recorded by the Kubernetes Metrics Server for the specified Kubernetes Nodes or all nodes in the cluster", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"label_selector": {"type": "string", "description": "Kubernetes label selector (e.g. 'node-role.kubernetes.io/worker=') to filter nodes by label (Optional, only applicable when name is not provided)", "pattern": "^([/_.\-A-Za-z0-9=, ()!])+$"}, "name": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the Node to get the resource consumption from (Optional, all Nodes if not provided)"}}}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_delete", "description": "Delete a Kubernetes Pod in the current or provided namespace with the provided name", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"name": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the Pod to delete"}, "namespace": {"type": "string", "description": "Namespace to delete the Pod from"}}, "required": ["name"]}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_exec", "description": "Execute a command in a Kubernetes Pod (shell access, run commands in container) in the current or provided namespace with the provided name and command", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"command": {"type": "array", "items": {"type": "string"}, "description": "Command to execute in the Pod container. The first item is the command to be run, and the rest are the arguments to that command. Example: ["ls", "-l", "/tmp"]"}, "container": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the Pod container where the command will be executed (Optional)"}, "name": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the Pod where the command will be executed"}, "namespace": {"type": "string", "description": "Namespace of the Pod where the command will be executed"}}, "required": ["name", "command"]}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_get", "description": "Get a Kubernetes Pod in the current or provided namespace with the provided name", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"name": {"type": "string", "description": "Name of the Pod"}, "namespace": {"type": "string", "description": "Namespace to get the Pod from"}}, "required": ["name"]}}}, {"type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_list", "description": "List all the Kubernetes pods in the current cluster from all namespaces", "parameters": {"type": "object", "properties": {"fieldSelector": {"type": "string", "description": "Optional Kubernetes field selector to filter pods by field values (e.g. 'status.phase=Running', 'spec.nodeName=node1'). Supported fields: metadata.name, metadata.namespace, spec.nodeName, spec.restartPolicy, spec.schedulerName, spec.serviceAccountName, status.phase (Pending/Running/Succeeded/Failed/Unknown), status.podIP, status.nominatedNodeName. name: litellm-acompletion
input:
{"messages": [{"role": "system", "content": "You are an ITOps analysis agent with access to Kubernetes tools via MCP.

Your task is to analyze the health of your assigned Kubernetes scope by:
1. Using the available tools to gather information
2. Investigating any issues you discover
3. Producing a final structured analysis

## Your Scope
You operate inside a single Kubernetes namespace: **`sock-shop`**.
The MCP server backing your tools is bound by RBAC to this namespace only.
You MUST stay within this scope:
- Always pass `namespace="sock-shop"` to any tool whose inputSchema declares a `namespace` argument.
- Prefer tools whose schema declares a `namespace` parameter.
- Do NOT call cluster-scoped tools — any tool whose schema does NOT accept `namespace`,
  including cluster-resource listers and node-level resource probes. They will fail
  with RBAC `forbidden`.
- For timeseries queries, pin `{namespace="sock-shop"}` selectors.

## Fallback Doctrine
The full inventory of tools you can call is supplied to you as `tools=[...]`.
Inspect each tool's `name`, `description`, and `inputSchema` before deciding
which one to call — argument names and tool naming vary across MCP servers,
so DO NOT assume any specific tool exists; pick by shape and description.

When a tool returns `forbidden`, do NOT retry it.
When a tool returns `not available` (or a similar "backend missing" error), do
NOT give up: scan your remaining tools and find one that can answer the same
question through a different route (e.g. if a snapshot tool is unavailable,
look for a tool that accepts a query string and ask a timeseries backend).
Only record an observability gap if you have tried every reasonable
alternative tool and all of them have failed.

## Resource Metrics
Primary path — find a tool that returns a per-pod resource snapshot
(CPU / memory) and accepts `namespace="sock-shop"`. Call it, and if it returns rows, use them.

Fallback path — if the primary tool is unavailable, returns `not available`,
or no such tool exists, find a tool that accepts a query string and ask a
Prometheus-compatible timeseries backend. Pin every query to your namespace:
  - CPU per pod:    sum by (pod) (rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"}[2m]))
  - Memory per pod: sum by (pod) (container_memory_working_set_bytes{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"})
  - Net RX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_receive_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
  - Net TX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_transmit_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
    Drop counters are not always emitted; if empty, retry with
    container_network_receive_errors_total / container_network_transmit_errors_total.
  - Pod state:      kube_pod_status_phase{namespace="sock-shop"} == 1
  - Restarts:       kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{namespace="sock-shop"}

Only record `metrics_unavailable` if BOTH paths fail — no snapshot tool AND no
timeseries-query tool can answer, OR every query returns empty for ≥ 2 attempts.

## Chaos Awareness (in-scope, data-driven detection)
You operate inside ONE namespace. You MUST NOT call any tool with a different
`namespace` argument. Before recommending remediation for any symptom, attempt
to determine whether the symptom is being driven by a deliberate disturbance
scheduled into your namespace. Use whatever tools are available to you:

1. Anomalous pod fleet — list pods in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`). The agent has NO inter-scan memory of pod inventory, so checks
   must be evaluable from a single snapshot:
     • pods with ownerReferences whose `kind` is NOT one of
       Deployment, ReplicaSet, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob — anything
       else (custom CRD kind) is suspicious
     • pods with NO ownerRef at all (raw pods are unusual in production namespaces)
     • pods whose containers terminate-and-restart in a tight loop
   Capture the pod name, the unfamiliar owner kind, and any annotation/label
   keys (NOT values — keys are enough to identify the framework at runtime).

2. In-namespace events — list events in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`) and look for reasons containing `Inject`, `Kill`, `Chaos`, `Disrupt`,
   `Fault`, or events referencing pods that no longer exist. Capture reasons verbatim.

3. Metric-backed disturbance probe — only if you have a tool that lists / introspects
   the metrics catalogue AND a tool that runs a query string against it:
   a. Discover available metrics whose name matches `(?i)(chaos|fault|inject|disrupt)`.
      If none, this step is empty.
   b. For each candidate series, read its label set. Pick the label whose name contains
      "namespace" or "ns" — that is the namespace-scoping label. DO NOT assume a
      fixed label name.
   c. Query `<series>{<discovered-label>="sock-shop"}` grouped by every other label.
      A non-empty result means a disturbance is currently targeting your namespace.

If ANY step surfaces evidence, classify the symptom as
`category="external-disturbance"` (or `"chaos-injected"` if a metric-backed step
surfaced matching series — the metric/label values are auditable evidence). Copy the
discovered marker into `summary`. Set `recommended_action` to monitor for recovery
rather than mutate the workload. Do NOT delete or scale the suspicious pods themselves.

If NO step surfaced evidence, treat the symptom as a real fault.

## Dependency Log Drill-Down
After identifying a problem pod, drill down using whatever tools are available:
  - fetch the pod's logs (use a tool that takes a pod `name` plus `namespace`;
    request ~300 lines of tail if a `tail` parameter is supported)
  - fetch the pod's full description (a tool that takes pod `name` + `namespace`).

If the pod's logs reference a connection-refused / no-reachable-servers / timeout
to a hostname `X`:
  - Match `X` against `<service>`, `<service>.sock-shop`, or `<service>.sock-shop.svc.cluster.local`.
    If matched, fetch the Service definition (use a tool that takes service `name`
    + `namespace`), read its `selector`, list pods in `sock-shop` whose labels match the
    selector, then fetch logs for each. All calls stay pinned to `namespace="sock-shop"`.
  - If `X` does not match any of those patterns, treat it as out-of-namespace —
    do NOT attempt to resolve it, do NOT read pods elsewhere; record `X` as an
    external dependency in `summary` and move on.

## When You Have Enough Information
Once you have gathered sufficient data to assess cluster health, provide your final analysis.

## JSON Output Format
Your final response must be a valid JSON object with this structure:
{
  "status_reasoning": {
    "determined_status": "healthy|degraded|critical|unknown",
    "status_justification": ["reason1", "reason2"],
    "data_quality": {
      "completeness": "complete|partial|insufficient",
      "gaps": ["metrics_unavailable", "logs_unavailable", ...],
      "confidence_impact": "Health score capped due to missing observability"
    }
  },
  "thoughts": {
    "key_observations": [],
    "analysis_approach": [],
    "observability_gaps": ["List any tools that failed or data that was unavailable"]
  },
  "health": {
    "total_pods": 0,
    "healthy_pods": 0,
    "unhealthy_pods": 0,
    "error_count": 0,
    "warning_count": 0,
    "overall_health_score": 0
  },
  "issues": [
    {
      "severity": "critical|warning|info",
      "component": "component-name",
      "category": "category",
      "summary": "description",
      "recommended_action": "what to do"
    }
  ],
  "insights": {
    "summary": "overall summary",
    "concerns": [],
    "recommendations": [],
    "observability_recommendations": ["Deploy metrics-server", ...]
  }
}"}, {"role": "user", "content": "Analyze the health of namespace `sock-shop` using only namespace-scoped tools. Task: Analyse the data from MCP tools and provide insights."}, {"role": "assistant", "content": null, "tool_calls": [{"id": "call_I4nsG8AlBi4CNiO2iUVvvumz", "type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_list_in_namespace", "arguments": "{"namespace": "sock-shop"}"}}, {"id": "call_GGB4PAkkcjp241SKAr9WPRz4", "type": "function", "function": {"name": "events_list", "arguments": "{"namespace": "sock-shop"}"}}, {"id": "call_JTM56OqhxZ9g5BE5PIeWkfBZ", "type": "function", "function": {"name": "pods_top", "arguments": "{"all_namespaces": false, "namespace": "sock-shop"}"}}, {"id": "call_XxHNHJz9EgiTRfCFIQAgXgIF", "type": "function", "function": {"name": "list_metrics", "arguments": "{"limit": 50, "offset": 0, "filter_pattern": "chaos|fault|inject|disrupt", "refresh_cache": false}"}}]}, {"role": "tool", "tool_call_id": "call_I4nsG8AlBi4CNiO2iUVvvumz", "content": "NAMESPACE   APIVERSION   KIND   NAME                                     READY   STATUS    RESTARTS        AGE   IP             NODE                                NOMINATED NODE   READINESS GATES   LABELS

name: litellm-acompletion
input:
{"messages": [{"role": "system", "content": "You are an ITOps analysis agent with access to Kubernetes tools via MCP.

Your task is to analyze the health of your assigned Kubernetes scope by:
1. Using the available tools to gather information
2. Investigating any issues you discover
3. Producing a final structured analysis

## Your Scope
You operate inside a single Kubernetes namespace: **`sock-shop`**.
The MCP server backing your tools is bound by RBAC to this namespace only.
You MUST stay within this scope:
- Always pass `namespace="sock-shop"` to any tool whose inputSchema declares a `namespace` argument.
- Prefer tools whose schema declares a `namespace` parameter.
- Do NOT call cluster-scoped tools — any tool whose schema does NOT accept `namespace`,
  including cluster-resource listers and node-level resource probes. They will fail
  with RBAC `forbidden`.
- For timeseries queries, pin `{namespace="sock-shop"}` selectors.

## Fallback Doctrine
The full inventory of tools you can call is supplied to you as `tools=[...]`.
Inspect each tool's `name`, `description`, and `inputSchema` before deciding
which one to call — argument names and tool naming vary across MCP servers,
so DO NOT assume any specific tool exists; pick by shape and description.

When a tool returns `forbidden`, do NOT retry it.
When a tool returns `not available` (or a similar "backend missing" error), do
NOT give up: scan your remaining tools and find one that can answer the same
question through a different route (e.g. if a snapshot tool is unavailable,
look for a tool that accepts a query string and ask a timeseries backend).
Only record an observability gap if you have tried every reasonable
alternative tool and all of them have failed.

## Resource Metrics
Primary path — find a tool that returns a per-pod resource snapshot
(CPU / memory) and accepts `namespace="sock-shop"`. Call it, and if it returns rows, use them.

Fallback path — if the primary tool is unavailable, returns `not available`,
or no such tool exists, find a tool that accepts a query string and ask a
Prometheus-compatible timeseries backend. Pin every query to your namespace:
  - CPU per pod:    sum by (pod) (rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"}[2m]))
  - Memory per pod: sum by (pod) (container_memory_working_set_bytes{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"})
  - Net RX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_receive_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
  - Net TX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_transmit_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
    Drop counters are not always emitted; if empty, retry with
    container_network_receive_errors_total / container_network_transmit_errors_total.
  - Pod state:      kube_pod_status_phase{namespace="sock-shop"} == 1
  - Restarts:       kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{namespace="sock-shop"}

Only record `metrics_unavailable` if BOTH paths fail — no snapshot tool AND no
timeseries-query tool can answer, OR every query returns empty for ≥ 2 attempts.

## Chaos Awareness (in-scope, data-driven detection)
You operate inside ONE namespace. You MUST NOT call any tool with a different
`namespace` argument. Before recommending remediation for any symptom, attempt
to determine whether the symptom is being driven by a deliberate disturbance
scheduled into your namespace. Use whatever tools are available to you:

1. Anomalous pod fleet — list pods in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`). The agent has NO inter-scan memory of pod inventory, so checks
   must be evaluable from a single snapshot:
     • pods with ownerReferences whose `kind` is NOT one of
       Deployment, ReplicaSet, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob — anything
       else (custom CRD kind) is suspicious
     • pods with NO ownerRef at all (raw pods are unusual in production namespaces)
     • pods whose containers terminate-and-restart in a tight loop
   Capture the pod name, the unfamiliar owner kind, and any annotation/label
   keys (NOT values — keys are enough to identify the framework at runtime).

2. In-namespace events — list events in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`) and look for reasons containing `Inject`, `Kill`, `Chaos`, `Disrupt`,
   `Fault`, or events referencing pods that no longer exist. Capture reasons verbatim.

3. Metric-backed disturbance probe — only if you have a tool that lists / introspects
   the metrics catalogue AND a tool that runs a query string against it:
   a. Discover available metrics whose name matches `(?i)(chaos|fault|inject|disrupt)`.
      If none, this step is empty.
   b. For each candidate series, read its label set. Pick the label whose name contains
      "namespace" or "ns" — that is the namespace-scoping label. DO NOT assume a
      fixed label name.
   c. Query `<series>{<discovered-label>="sock-shop"}` grouped by every other label.
      A non-empty result means a disturbance is currently targeting your namespace.

If ANY step surfaces evidence, classify the symptom as
`category="external-disturbance"` (or `"chaos-injected"` if a metric-backed step
surfaced matching series — the metric/label values are auditable evidence). Copy the
discovered marker into `summary`. Set `recommended_action` to monitor for recovery
rather than mutate the workload. Do NOT delete or scale the suspicious pods themselves.

If NO step surfaced evidence, treat the symptom as a real fault.

## Dependency Log Drill-Down
After identifying a problem pod, drill down using whatever tools are available:
  - fetch the pod's logs (use a tool that takes a pod `name` plus `namespace`;
    request ~300 lines of tail if a `tail` parameter is supported)
  - fetch the pod's full description (a tool that takes pod `name` + `namespace`).

If the pod's logs reference a connection-refused / no-reachable-servers / timeout
to a hostname `X`:
  - Match `X` against `<service>`, `<service>.sock-shop`, or `<service>.sock-shop.svc.cluster.local`.
    If matched, fetch the Service definition (use a tool that takes service `name`
    + `namespace`), read its `selector`, list pods in `sock-shop` whose labels match the
    selector, then fetch logs for each. All calls stay pinned to `namespace="sock-shop"`.
  - If `X` does not match any of those patterns, treat it as out-of-namespace —
    do NOT attempt to resolve it, do NOT read pods elsewhere; record `X` as an
    external dependency in `summary` and move on.

## When You Have Enough Information
Once you have gathered sufficient data to assess cluster health, provide your final analysis.

## JSON Output Format
Your final response must be a valid JSON object with this structure:
{
  "status_reasoning": {
    "determined_status": "healthy|degraded|critical|unknown",
    "status_justification": ["reason1", "reason2"],
    "data_quality": {
      "completeness": "complete|partial|insufficient",
