name: litellm-acompletion
input:
{"messages": [{"role": "system", "content": "You are an ITOps analysis agent with access to Kubernetes tools via MCP.

Your task is to analyze the health of your assigned Kubernetes scope by:
1. Using the available tools to gather information
2. Investigating any issues you discover
3. Producing a final structured analysis

## Your Scope
You operate inside a single Kubernetes namespace: **`sock-shop`**.
The MCP server backing your tools is bound by RBAC to this namespace only.
You MUST stay within this scope:
- Always pass `namespace="sock-shop"` to any tool whose inputSchema declares a `namespace` argument.
- Prefer tools whose schema declares a `namespace` parameter.
- Do NOT call cluster-scoped tools — any tool whose schema does NOT accept `namespace`,
  including cluster-resource listers and node-level resource probes. They will fail
  with RBAC `forbidden`.
- For timeseries queries, pin `{namespace="sock-shop"}` selectors.

## Fallback Doctrine
The full inventory of tools you can call is supplied to you as `tools=[...]`.
Inspect each tool's `name`, `description`, and `inputSchema` before deciding
which one to call — argument names and tool naming vary across MCP servers,
so DO NOT assume any specific tool exists; pick by shape and description.

When a tool returns `forbidden`, do NOT retry it.
When a tool returns `not available` (or a similar "backend missing" error), do
NOT give up: scan your remaining tools and find one that can answer the same
question through a different route (e.g. if a snapshot tool is unavailable,
look for a tool that accepts a query string and ask a timeseries backend).
Only record an observability gap if you have tried every reasonable
alternative tool and all of them have failed.

## Resource Metrics
Primary path — find a tool that returns a per-pod resource snapshot
(CPU / memory) and accepts `namespace="sock-shop"`. Call it, and if it returns rows, use them.

Fallback path — if the primary tool is unavailable, returns `not available`,
or no such tool exists, find a tool that accepts a query string and ask a
Prometheus-compatible timeseries backend. Pin every query to your namespace:
  - CPU per pod:    sum by (pod) (rate(container_cpu_usage_seconds_total{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"}[2m]))
  - Memory per pod: sum by (pod) (container_memory_working_set_bytes{namespace="sock-shop",container!="",container!="POD"})
  - Net RX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_receive_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
  - Net TX drops:   sum by (pod) (rate(container_network_transmit_packets_dropped_total{namespace="sock-shop"}[2m]))
    Drop counters are not always emitted; if empty, retry with
    container_network_receive_errors_total / container_network_transmit_errors_total.
  - Pod state:      kube_pod_status_phase{namespace="sock-shop"} == 1
  - Restarts:       kube_pod_container_status_restarts_total{namespace="sock-shop"}

Only record `metrics_unavailable` if BOTH paths fail — no snapshot tool AND no
timeseries-query tool can answer, OR every query returns empty for ≥ 2 attempts.

## Chaos Awareness (in-scope, data-driven detection)
You operate inside ONE namespace. You MUST NOT call any tool with a different
`namespace` argument. Before recommending remediation for any symptom, attempt
to determine whether the symptom is being driven by a deliberate disturbance
scheduled into your namespace. Use whatever tools are available to you:

1. Anomalous pod fleet — list pods in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`). The agent has NO inter-scan memory of pod inventory, so checks
   must be evaluable from a single snapshot:
     • pods with ownerReferences whose `kind` is NOT one of
       Deployment, ReplicaSet, StatefulSet, DaemonSet, Job, CronJob — anything
       else (custom CRD kind) is suspicious
     • pods with NO ownerRef at all (raw pods are unusual in production namespaces)
     • pods whose containers terminate-and-restart in a tight loop
   Capture the pod name, the unfamiliar owner kind, and any annotation/label
   keys (NOT values — keys are enough to identify the framework at runtime).

2. In-namespace events — list events in `sock-shop` (use a tool whose schema accepts
   `namespace`) and look for reasons containing `Inject`, `Kill`, `Chaos`, `Disrupt`,
   `Fault`, or events referencing pods that no longer exist. Capture reasons verbatim.

3. Metric-backed disturbance probe — only if you have a tool that lists / introspects
   the metrics catalogue AND a tool that runs a query string against it:
   a. Discover available metrics whose name matches `(?i)(chaos|fault|inject|disrupt)`.
      If none, this step is empty.
   b. For each candidate series, read its label set. Pick the label whose name contains
      "namespace" or "ns" — that is the namespace-scoping label. DO NOT assume a
      fixed label name.
   c. Query `<series>{<discovered-label>="sock-shop"}` grouped by every other label.
      A non-empty result means a disturbance is currently targeting your namespace.

If ANY step surfaces evidence, classify the symptom as
`category="external-disturbance"` (or `"chaos-injected"` if a metric-backed step
surfaced matching series — the metric/label values are auditable evidence). Copy the
discovered marker into `summary`. Set `recommended_action` to monitor for recovery
rather than mutate the workload. Do NOT delete or scale the suspicious pods themselves.

If NO step surfaced evidence, treat the symptom as a real fault.

## Dependency Log Drill-Down
After identifying a problem pod, drill down using whatever tools are available:
  - fetch the pod's logs (use a tool that takes a pod `name` plus `namespace`;
    request ~300 lines of tail if a `tail` parameter is supported)
  - fetch the pod's full description (a tool that takes pod `name` + `namespace`).

If the pod's logs reference a connection-refused / no-reachable-servers / timeout
to a hostname `X`:
  - Match `X` against `<service>`, `<service>.sock-shop`, or `<service>.sock-shop.svc.cluster.local`.
    If matched, fetch the Service definition (use a tool that takes service `name`
    + `namespace`), read its `selector`, list pods in `sock-shop` whose labels match the
    selector, then fetch logs for each. All calls stay pinned to `namespace="sock-shop"`.
  - If `X` does not match any of those patterns, treat it as out-of-namespace —
    do NOT attempt to resolve it, do NOT read pods elsewhere; record `X` as an
    external dependency in `summary` and move on.

## When You Have Enough Information
Once you have gathered sufficient data to assess cluster health, provide your final analysis.

{"content": "{
  "status_reasoning": {
    "determined_status": "degraded",
    "status_justification": [
      "All 16 pods in namespace sock-shop are in Running phase and Ready, with no CrashLoopBackOff or Pending pods.",
      "There are Warning events for carts startup probe failures (connection refused / timeout on /health) and non‑zero restarts on flash-agent and user pods, indicating past instability even though they are currently Running.",
      "Presence of a flash-agent pod (custom app=flash-agent) suggests an external/chaos-style agent is deployed in the namespace, but no explicit chaos/fault events were found; current impact appears low."
    ],
    "data_quality": {
      "completeness": "partial",
      "gaps": [
        "metrics_unavailable",
        "events_truncated",
        "no_metric_catalogue_tool"
      ],
      "confidence_impact": "Health score slightly reduced due to missing live CPU/memory metrics and truncated events; however, pod and event data are sufficient for a reasonable assessment."
    }
  },
  "thoughts": {
    "key_observations": [
      "16 pods listed in sock-shop; all show STATUS=Running and READY 1/1 (or 2/2 for flash-agent).",
      "No pods are Pending, Failed, or in CrashLoopBackOff; restart counts are low (flash-agent: 2, user: 1).",
      "Events show normal scheduling, image pulls, container creation and start for core sock-shop services and databases.",
      "carts pod experienced startup probe failures (connection refused and timeout on /health) early in its lifecycle but is now Running.",
      "A flash-agent pod with app=flash-agent label is present, which is not a standard sock-shop component and likely represents an external/chaos/agent framework.",
      "No events contain obvious chaos-related reasons such as Inject, Kill, Chaos, Disrupt, or Fault in the visible (non-truncated) portion.",
      "Metrics server access for pod-level CPU/memory (pods.metrics.k8s.io) is forbidden for the MCP service account, so resource usage cannot be directly assessed.",
      "Prometheus metric catalogue tool (list_metrics) is not available, preventing metric-backed chaos detection."
    ],
    "analysis_approach": [
      "Listed all pods in namespace sock-shop and inspected their status, readiness, restarts, and labels.",
      "Reviewed namespace-scoped events for sock-shop to identify warnings, failures, and any chaos-related markers.",
      "Attempted to retrieve pod-level resource metrics via pods_top, which failed due to RBAC on metrics.k8s.io.",
      "Attempted to discover chaos/fault-related metrics via a metric catalogue tool, which is not available.",
      "Assessed potential external disturbance by correlating the presence of flash-agent with events and restarts.",
      "Synthesized an overall health view based on pod states, events, and limited restart information."
    ],
    "observability_gaps": [
      "functions.pods_top: forbidden on pods.metrics.k8s.io for namespace sock-shop due to RBAC on the MCP service account.",
      "functions.list_metrics: tool not available in this MCP server, so no metric catalogue or chaos metric discovery.",
      "events_list output is truncated, so some later events (including for flash-agent and others) are not fully visible."
    ]
  },
  "health": {
    "total_pods": 16,
    "healthy_pods": 16,
    "unhealthy_pods": 0,
    "error_count": 0,
    "warning_count": 1,
    "overall_health_score": 85
  },
  "issues": [
    {
      "severity": "warning",
      "component": "carts",
      "category": "application-health",
      "summary": "carts pod experienced startup probe failures on /health (connection refused and timeout) shortly after deployment, though it is now Running and Ready.",
      "recommended_action": "Review the carts service startup and health-check implementation. Confirm that the application binds to the expected port before the startup probe window and that /health is responsive under load. Consider adjusting startupProbe initialDelaySeconds, periodSeconds, or failureThreshold if the app legitimately needs more warm-up time, and monitor for any recurrence of Unhealthy events."
    },
    {
      "severity": "warning",
      "component": "user",
      "category": "application-health",
      "summary": "user pod shows a non-zero restart count (1 restart ~13 minutes ago) despite currently being Running and Ready.",
      "recommended_action": "Inspect recent logs for the user pod around the time of the restart to identify the cause (e.g., crash, OOM, configuration issue). If the restart was due to a transient condition and has not recurred, continue to monitor; if it recurs, capture logs and consider adding more robust error handling or resource adjustments."
    },
    {
      "severity": "info",
      "component": "flash-agent",
      "category": "external-disturbance",
      "summary": "flash-agent pod (app=flash-agent) is running in the sock-shop namespace with 2 restarts; this appears to be an external agent or chaos/validation component rather than a core sock-shop service.",
      "recommended_action": "Confirm with the owning team whether flash-agent is an intentional deployment (e.g., chaos/validation/monitoring agent) and whether any experiments or disturbances are currently scheduled. Do not modify or delete this pod without coordination. Monitor for any events or logs indicating that it is injecting faults into sock-shop services."
    },
    {
      "severity": "info",
      "component": "namespace/sock-shop",
      "category": "observability",
      "summary": "Pod-level CPU and memory metrics are not accessible due to RBAC restrictions on metrics.k8s.io for the MCP service account.",
      "recommended_action": "If fine-grained resource health is required, update RBAC to allow the MCP service account (system:serviceaccount:litmus:mcp-server) to list pods.metrics.k8s.io in namespace sock-shop, or provide an alternative namespace-scoped metrics endpoint. Until then, rely on pod status, events, and application-level telemetry for capacity assessment."
    }
  ],
  "insights": {
    "summary": "The sock-shop namespace is generally healthy: all pods are Running and Ready, with low restart counts and mostly Normal events. There are minor historical issues (startup probe failures on carts and a single restart on user) and the presence of a flash-agent pod suggests an external agent or potential disturbance framework, but there is no clear evidence of active chaos injection or ongoing faults. Overall health is assessed as degraded rather than fully healthy due to these warnings and limited observability into resource metrics.",
    "concerns": [
      "Historical startup probe failures on the carts service indicate that its readiness during startup may be fragile or tightly coupled to probe timing.",
      "Non-zero restarts on user and flash-agent pods warrant log review to ensure there are no recurring or latent issues.",
      "Lack of access to pod-level CPU/memory metrics prevents verification of resource headroom and may hide early signs of saturation.",
      "The flash-agent pod, while likely intentional, introduces uncertainty about potential external disturbances in the namespace."
    ],
    "recommendations": [
      "For carts: Review and, if necessary, tune startupProbe configuration and application startup sequence so that /health is reliably available within the probe window. Add monitoring on startup probe failures to catch regressions.",
      "For user: Retrieve and inspect recent logs around the time of the restart to confirm root cause. If the restart was due to a known transient issue, document it; if unknown, add additional logging or instrumentation.",
      "For flash-agent: Validate ownership and purpose (e.g., chaos/validation agent). If it is used for experiments, ensure experiments are documented and that their schedules are visible to operators monitoring sock-shop.",
      "Implement or verify application-level health and performance dashboards (e.g., HTTP error rates, latency, and dependency health) for core sock-shop services (front-end, carts, catalogue, orders, payment, shipping, user).",
      "Establish alerting on pod restarts and Unhealthy events in sock-shop so that similar issues are detected quickly in the future."
    ],
    "observability_recommendations": [
      "Grant the MCP service account (system:serviceaccount:litmus:mcp-server) read access to pods.metrics.k8s.io in namespace sock-shop so tools like pods_top can return CPU and memory usage per pod.",
      "If Prometheus is available, expose a namespace-scoped metric query tool so that chaos/fault-related metrics and resource usage can be inspected without cluster-wide permissions.",
      "Ensure event retention and retrieval are sufficient so that full event histories (including for flash-agent and other pods) are not truncated during analysis.",
      "Encourage services in sock-shop to emit structured logs and basic SLI metrics (latency, error rate, throughput) to complement Kubernetes-level health signals."
    ]
  }
